[en] Yeah. So funding rounds is really interesting. When you're running a company, yeah, there's typically—I mean, you probably know this. There's your angel rounds, maybe your friends and family get involved.

Speaker 2:

[en] But what's the rounds you do? You say, "I'm starting my company. I'm just asking for this much as my first round to reach this level." And then when I reach this level, I'm going for a second round kind of thing.

Speaker 1:

[en] Exactly. Typically, there's different ways to do it. Usually, you want to raise enough money in a given round to last at least 18 to 24 months because fundraising is very time-consuming, and you don't want to spend too much of your time raising money. Yeah, you need to be building product, building your market. So they typically say, "Raise enough on the projected cost or the burn rate to last you 18 to 24 months." So that's maybe your minimum. And then you want to look at other companies that are maybe comparable. You can benchmark your valuation against theirs. You don't want to give away more than, ideally, 15%, but maximum 20% of your company in a given funding round because most companies that raise capital, they need to raise multiple rounds. And so after a few rounds of giving away 20%, you're not left with very much. So founders can sometimes—because investors also want to make sure that founders are incentivized to stay excited about the business. So there can be reallocations of shares for founders at a later stage. So there is some of that that can happen, but you don't want to give away too much. In Southeast Asia, especially in the early days of the ecosystem, you would regularly see bright-eyed entrepreneurs starting their first company saying, "I need capital to do this thing," and an investor would take advantage of that and say, "Okay, I want to own 51% of your company."

Speaker 2:

[en] But short it right away.

Speaker 1:

[en] And give you some small little bit of money. And I'm going to call you every day saying, "What's going on? What's going on? What's going on?" So then their life becomes miserable, and inevitably, the business fails. It doesn't serve anyone well. So those are some of the teething pains of a maturing ecosystem. But yeah, typically, it's like that. So when I'm raising capital, I'll look at what other peer companies are raising at. I'll also make sure we're not giving away too much to the company. And I want it to be enough capital so that we have enough runway so that I'm not constantly fundraising. So yeah.

Speaker 2:

[en] And what is your end goal? Do you go public or?

Speaker 1:

[en] Honestly, for me and my co-founder, we started the company because we really want to build a company that can change the industry in a number of different ways, not only what we do, but also how we do it. And to do that, you typically need a company that is standing independently on its own two feet. That might mean a public listing, or it might mean some companies like SpaceX, for example. They're not public yet, but they've definitely changed how their industry works. So maybe we stay private for a long time. Something about going public that doesn't excite me, like quarterly earnings and constant audits, the compliance. And you see there's research on public companies. It doesn't need to be this way, but inevitably, it happens this way that because the reporting schedule is so short-term, the incentives within the company get skewed short-term. So then their ability to think long-term is affected. And ideally, you shouldn't need to do that. You should still be thinking long-term. So there's some companies which have been able to resist some of that, but most companies, they just become short-term. Yeah.

Speaker 2:

[en] You really need to be kind of established enough to really separate the short-term requirements with your long-term views.

Speaker 1:

[en] Exactly. Yeah. And build that culture that people aren't checking the stock price every day. It's like, "Hey, independent of the ups and downs," because every day it's going to be some up and some down. And oftentimes, it's not about you. It's about the broader market and whatever tweet went out that day. And so you need your employees not checking that stuff, but thinking long-term.
